headline: 8,477 stations are listed under the Ministry of Railways as of December 2022.
But 24 of those rows are exact duplicates — same name, same code, listed twice. The real count is closer to 8,453.
Nearly every duplicate traces back to one division: Samastipur (SPJ), Bihar — its sheet appears to have been pasted into the national file twice.
8,441 unique station codes exist across the list — fewer than the number of rows, because a handful of codes are shared.
16 railway zones appear in this list. Two zones that officially exist today — Konkan Railway and South Coast Railway — don't appear at all; they're run as separate corporate entities.
Goa shows only 9 stations in this Ministry list, because its main line, the Konkan Railway, isn't part of this dataset.
66 operating divisions run the entire network, reporting up through those 16 zones.
Northern Railway is the single biggest zone by station count: 1,015 stations.
West Central Railway is the smallest zone: 297 stations.
Ferozepur (FZR) division alone runs 249 stations — the busiest division in the country.
Raipur ('R') division in Chhattisgarh runs just 50 — the smallest division nationally.
Northern Railway's divisions average 203 stations each, more than double South Eastern Railway's average of 93 — the same zone rank, wildly different workloads.
4,194 stations — 49.5%, essentially one in every two — are NSG6, the lowest non-suburban tier: under ₹1 crore a year, under a million passengers.
1,577 stations are HG3, the lowest halt grade of all — under ₹5 lakh a year.
Combined, 68.1% of the entire network sits in the bottom two tiers alone.
Only 22 stations nationwide — 0.26% — are NSG1, the top tier, requiring over ₹500 crore a year and 20 million+ passengers.
Group every station into elite, workhorse, or long tail, and the split is 22 : 712 : 7,743.
91.3% of Indian Railways, by station count, is 'long tail' — low traffic, low revenue, essential anyway.
The gap between the top tier's earnings floor (₹500 crore) and the bottom tier's ceiling (₹5 lakh) spans roughly four orders of magnitude, on one national ladder.
Central Railway has the healthiest mix of any zone — only 81.5% long-tail, the lowest share in the country.
Northeast Frontier Railway has the highest long-tail share of any zone: 94.5%.
Uttar Pradesh has more stations than any other state: 1,173 — 13.8% of the entire national network.
Uttar Pradesh alone has more stations than the eight smallest states and union territories combined (20).
The top 5 states by station count hold 4,015 stations between them — 47.4% of the whole country.
Sikkim has zero stations in this list.
Mizoram has exactly one station: Bhairabi, graded NSG6.
Meghalaya has exactly one station too: Mendipathar, also NSG6.
Nagaland has three stations. Arunachal Pradesh has three.
Those five states combined — Sikkim, Mizoram, Meghalaya, Nagaland, Arunachal Pradesh — add up to 8 stations. Ferozepur division alone has 31 times that many.
Jammu & Kashmir has only 34 stations, and 25 of them (73.5%) are the lowest NSG6 tier.
28 of India's 592 districts — nearly 1 in 20 — have exactly one railway station.
3 stations in the entire national list are missing a district value altogether.
Patna district has more stations than any other single district in the country: 71.
Hooghly district in West Bengal is a close second at 66 — denser, station for station, than Mumbai district (56).
Four of the ten most rail-dense districts in the country are in West Bengal.
Delhi has only 46 stations total, but 3 of them are NSG1 — a 10.9% elite ratio, by far the highest of any state.
Maharashtra holds 13 of the national elite-or-near-elite (NSG1+NSG2) stations, more than any other state.
7 of India's 22 top-tier stations sit within the greater Mumbai region alone.
Tamil Nadu's elite ratio is the second highest at 2.1% — still a fifth of Delhi's.
8 of India's 35 states and union territories have at least one NSG1 station. 27 have none.
India's formal 'suburban' (SG) station classification exists in only 4 states — West Bengal, Maharashtra, Tamil Nadu, Telangana. 28 states and UTs have no SG-classified stations at all.
Sealdah division alone runs 129 of West Bengal's 271 suburban-grade stations.
The word 'Halt' appears literally in 690 station names — 25 times more than the 26 stations formally graded HG1.
317 station names end in 'Road' — a habit from an era when the station was built as close to a town as terrain allowed, not necessarily in it.
'PUR' — a Sanskrit-rooted suffix meaning town — appears in 1,004 station names. Roughly 1 in 8 stations nationwide.
'GARH' (fort) shows up in 141 names. 'GANJ' (market town) in 135. 'KOT' (stronghold) in 84.
'PALLI' and 'PALLE' — South Indian village suffixes — appear a combined 77 times, almost entirely in the southern states.
43 stations are named '___ Kalan' (greater) and 20 '___ Khurd' (lesser) — a Persian-derived twin-village naming convention still alive on the railway map.
Not one of the 446 junction stations spells out the full word 'Junction' in its printed name. Every one abbreviates to 'Jn.'
Only 13 stations carry 'Cantt' — marking historic military garrison towns.
Puducherry's station names are the longest in the country on average: 16.25 characters.
Tamil Nadu is a close second at 13.7 characters average — both far above Gujarat's lean 8.5-character average.
Only 2 stations in the entire national list contain a digit in their name — both obscure survey-marker names in Jharkhand: 'TP No. 6/3' and 'TP No. 7/11'.
Not a single station name anywhere in the list contains an apostrophe.
No station name in the country is reused more than twice.
Just 6 station codes are a single letter long: D, J, G, R, S, Y.
One of them — Y, for Yeliyur, an obscure Karnataka halt — holds one of the rarest identifiers in the whole system despite being a minor NSG6 stop.
201 station codes are palindromes, reading the same forwards and backwards — including Cuttack's real code, CTC.
A handful of codes accidentally spell real English words: GO, ON, BE, TO, and BIG.
B is the single most common starting letter for both station names and station codes — 1,063 of each.
Only 4 codes in the entire system start with X, and just 10 start with Q.
The shortest station name in the country is two letters long: IB, in Odisha.
12 station codes are shared by two differently-named entries — codes are supposed to be nationally unique.
Most of those are typo pairs of the same real place: 'BADLA GHAT' vs 'BADLAGHAT', 'LAHERIA SARAI' vs 'LAHERIASARAI', 'DARBANGA' vs 'DARBHANGA JN.' — almost all from Samastipur division again.

But three are genuine collisions between real, unrelated stations hundreds of kilometers apart: Bara (Madhya Pradesh) and Bilara (Rajasthan) share a code. So do Kattur (Tamil Nadu) and Kottur (Telangana).
Mumbai's Dadar is listed twice under two different codes — DR and DDR — one per railway zone that touches it.
136 stations have no historical (pre-2017) category recorded at all — a blank field in an otherwise complete national dataset.

Another 191 rows carry stray, non-standard text in that same column — including the literal word 'New', a lone dash character, and one row where 'NSG6' (a value from a completely different column) got typed in by mistake.
Altogether, about 2.3% of the dataset carries a typo, blank, or copy-paste artifact in just that one column.
The state field has miscapitalized duplicates too — 'Karnataka' appears as a separate value from 'KARNATAKA', and the same happens for West Bengal and Odisha.
One station, Navapur, is listed with its state field reading 'Maharashtra/Gujarat' — because its platform genuinely straddles the two states' border.
36 station names appear twice each somewhere in the country — including two stations both named Srinagar: one the Kashmir capital, one an unrelated stop in Rajasthan.
CSMT — Mumbai, Maharashtra
detail: One of India's 22 NSG1 stations, the country's top commercial tier.
